strengths: Detailed client investigation with quantified problem context and multiple consultations
Clear scenario description linking client needs to system requirements
Comprehensive set of nine success criteria directly tied to client needs
weaknesses: Rationale lacks comparative analysis of alternative solutions
Technology justification is surface-level with an incomplete server-side explanation
A few success criteria remain qualitative rather than fully measurable
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Scenario and client consultation are described in depth (band 6), success criteria are thorough (band 5), but the product rationale is solid yet not exhaustively justified (band 4). Overall this balances to 5 out of 6.

strengths: Moderate complexity: role-based access, JWT auth, analytics and email notifications
Effective use of Node.js, React, MongoDB, Chart.js and security libraries
Original full-stack implementation showing solid ingenuity in problem-solving
weaknesses: Documentation and source attribution are minimal; external tutorials are not cited
Techniques such as error handling are not fully documented in pseudocode
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The solution demonstrates moderate-high complexity and makes appropriate, effective use of tools (bands 5â8), but the sparse attribution and technique explanation prevent it reaching the very top of 12. A score of 9/12 reflects this mix.

strengths: Extensive, dated and linked record of over 40 development tasks
Comprehensive design overview including flowcharts, use-case diagram, wireframes and schema
Detailed test plan covering multiple modules with methods and expected results
weaknesses: A handful of task entries are blank or incomplete
Several test-plan expected outcomes are truncated
Design diagrams could annotate exception paths
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands fully meet top-band descriptors (tasks and design overview at band 6), and the test plan is very detailed with only minor gaps (band 5). Holistically this merits the full 6.

strengths: Design mock-ups and descriptions show intended product functionality
Modular RESTful architecture suggests potential for extension
weaknesses: No working screenshots or video evidence to confirm functionality
Extensibility is implied but no concrete extension points or examples are provided
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Functionality is only partially demonstrated without live evidence (band 1â2), and while the design is modular, extensions would require significant effort (band 1â2). The work sits at 2 out of 4.

weaknesses: No evaluation mapping features to success criteria
No client or adviser feedback provided
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands (evaluation against criteria, feedback, recommendations) are absent, yielding 0 out of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: Multiple flow-charts, a use-case diagram, low-fidelity wireframes, pseudocode and a full database schema together create a comprehensive, coherent overview of structure, navigation and data.  The documentation shows clear understanding of how the final product will be built, satisfying the top-band descriptor.

The rationale discusses why a bespoke web solution is preferable (cost, accessibility across devices, real-time updates, scalability).  Technologies are justified at a surface but clear level (Node/React/MongoDB with reasons).  However, depth is unevenâeconomic justification and comparison with alternatives are briefâso the explanation is solid but not fully exhaustive, fitting the 3-4 band.

The student employs React, Node.js, MongoDB, JWT, bcrypt, Chart.js and Git.  Usage is described appropriately (e.g., RESTful APIs, hashing, chart generation), indicating effective, relevant employment of existing tools though not pushing their limits.  This comfortably fits the 5-8 range.

The test plan contains extensive tables covering security, admin, client, dashboard and page-specific features with methods and expected outputs.  Some tests are repetitive and a few expected results are truncated, showing minor gaps, yet overall it is detailed and complete enough to merit a high scoreâ5 is an appropriate placement.
The submission does not contain a section outlining recommendations for future product improvements; consequently, this strand scores zero.

Models, routes and database structures are described, but external sources or tutorials are not cited, and explanation of techniques is moderate rather than comprehensive.  All work seems original yet attribution is minimal, so the strand lands in the 1-4 band at about 3.

The record of tasks spans 40+ entries, gives planned actions, outcomes, times, dates and links to criteria.  It traces the process from initial interviews through development, testing and evaluation, providing a clear picture of progression.  This meets all descriptors for a detailed and complete record.

A full-stack inventory system with role-based access, JWT authentication, dashboard analytics, CRUD operations and email notifications represents a moderate level of complexity.  It goes beyond basic CRUD but stops short of advanced algorithms or highly novel features, positioning it mid-band (5-8) at about 6.

Nine success criteria cover security, CRUD operations, analytics, alerts, scalability and stock thresholds.  Each criterion is specific, measurable and directly linked to the clientâs needs; the clientâs agreement is documented.  One or two criteria could be more quantifiable (e.g. response-time limits), so awarding 5 rather than 6 is appropriate within the comprehensive band.
Although the planning references future client feedback, no actual feedback evidence (quotes, screenshots, survey) appears in the submission, so this strand remains at zero.

The design implies modularity (separate pages, RESTful APIs), but without seeing code modularity or extension examples, ease of modification remains unclear.  Some expansion is possible yet would likely require notable effort, so a low score within the 1-2 band is justified.

The student clearly identifies a real client (Gled) and explains the clientâs current manual process and its shortcomings. Two dated consultations are referenced and specific evidence of what was discussed is summarised, demonstrating repeated, detailed interaction.  The level of detail (daily transaction volume, paper loss, over/under-stocking) and the description of the desired system constitute thorough evidence of consultation, placing the work firmly in the top band.

Functionality is described in prose and interface mock-ups, but no execution evidence (screenshots of a working build or video) is provided in the submission.  This suggests only partial demonstration at best, warranting the middle band and a cautious score of 2.
No section evaluating the finished product against the stated success criteria is included.  Therefore, this strand earns zero.

annotations.comment: The problem statement effectively quantifies Gledâs manual transaction volume and the vulnerabilities of paper records, demonstrating a thorough client investigation.
The rationale mentions cost and accessibility of a bespoke web solution but lacks comparative analysis against existing alternatives to fully justify the choice.
The technology justification stops midâsentence, leaving the reader unclear about the serverâside implementation detailsâensure all sentences are complete.
Nine success criteria cover security, CRUD, analytics, alerts, scalability, and thresholds, each directly linked to client needs with documented client agreement.
Several criteria (e.g., response times) remain qualitative; specify measurable targets (e.g., API latency <200 ms) to increase clarity and testability.
Consider annotating flowchart decision nodes with conditions (e.g., invalid login) to demonstrate handling of exceptions in the design overview.
Pseudocode begins clearly with server and model initialization, offering a structured approach to API design.
Model definitions lack consistency in parameter ordering (e.g., userID before imageUrl). Standardize ordering to improve readability and maintainability.
Error-handling pseudocode is missing. Include steps for validation failures (e.g., email format) to demonstrate comprehensive technique use.
Inventory page mock-up illustrates intended layout but lacks a real screenshot or video evidence of functional behavior.
Client/adviser feedback is absent. Incorporate direct quotes or survey summaries to substantiate Criterion E feedback.
Future recommendations are missing; suggest realistic enhancements (e.g., mobile offline support, advanced alerting) based on identified limitations.
The record of tasks table spans 44 entries with planned actions, outcomes, and dates; this comprehensive log shows clear project management.
Some task entries (e.g., #18) contain no data, suggesting incomplete documentation. Ensure all tasks are fully recorded or remove empty rows.
Flowchart 01 provides a clear high-level view of system navigation from login to dashboard, illustrating a complete process overview.
Flowchart 02 and 03 offer layered detail for user and inventory flows; the coherence between these diagrams clearly demonstrates product structure.
The use-case diagram effectively maps actors to system functions; however, adding alternative paths (e.g., forgotten password) would deepen the coverage.
The design-of-solution disclaimer clarifies prototype origins, but linking low-fidelity wireframes to final HTML/CSS outputs would strengthen traceability.
Without a video or build screenshots, it is difficult to verify full product functionality; include demonstration evidence in Criterion D deliverables.
Modularity is implied through separate pages, but without code examples of extension points, potential modifications remain unclear.
No section is provided evaluating the implemented product against the stated success criteria; a full mapping table is needed.
